subject: ap-chemistry
theme: Intermolecular forces, solution behavior, and gas properties emerge from the structure and polarity of molecules — understanding these links explains why substances mix, dissolve, and behave as they do under different conditions.

core_concepts.title: Types of Intermolecular Forces
core_concepts.detail: London dispersion forces: present in all molecules; increase with molar mass and surface area. Dipole-dipole forces: between polar molecules. Hydrogen bonding: N–H, O–H, or F–H interacting with N, O, or F; strongest type among the three. Stronger IMFs → higher boiling point, higher surface tension, higher viscosity.

core_concepts.title: Like Dissolves Like
core_concepts.detail: Polar and ionic solutes dissolve in polar solvents (e.g., water); nonpolar solutes dissolve in nonpolar solvents. Dissolution is favorable when solute-solvent IMFs are similar in strength to solute-solute and solvent-solvent IMFs. Strong hydrogen bonding in water makes it difficult to dissolve nonpolar solutes.

core_concepts.title: Molarity and Concentration
core_concepts.detail: Molarity ($M$) = moles of solute / liters of solution. The Beer-Lambert Law $A = \varepsilon l c$ relates absorbance ($A$) to molar absorptivity ($\varepsilon$), path length ($l$), and molarity ($c$). A linear calibration curve (absorbance vs. concentration) is used to determine unknown concentrations by spectrophotometry.

core_concepts.title: Ideal Gas Law
core_concepts.detail: The ideal gas law $PV = nRT$ combines Boyle's ($P \propto 1/V$), Charles's ($V \propto T$), and Avogadro's ($V \propto n$) laws. At STP (273 K, 1 atm), 1 mol of ideal gas occupies 22.4 L. Real gases deviate from ideality at high pressure (volume becomes significant) and low temperature (IMFs become significant).

core_concepts.title: Kinetic Molecular Theory
core_concepts.detail: KMT postulates: (1) gas molecules are tiny and far apart, (2) no intermolecular forces between molecules, (3) elastic collisions conserve kinetic energy, (4) average kinetic energy is proportional to absolute temperature. The Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution shows the spread of molecular speeds; heavier molecules have lower average speeds at the same temperature.

core_concepts.title: Colligative Properties
core_concepts.detail: Colligative properties depend on the number of solute particles, not their identity: boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure. Ionic compounds produce multiple ions per formula unit, multiplying their colligative effect by the van 't Hoff factor $i$.

core_concepts.title: Chromatography and Separation
core_concepts.detail: Chromatography separates mixtures based on differential affinity for stationary vs. mobile phases. The retention factor $R_f = d_{spot}/d_{solvent}$ indicates affinity for the mobile phase. Higher $R_f$ = more affinity for mobile phase (travels farther). Distillation separates based on boiling points; filtration separates heterogeneous mixtures.

formulas.name: Ideal gas law
formulas.description: Relates pressure ($P$ in atm), volume ($V$ in L), moles ($n$), gas constant ($R = 0.0821$ L·atm/mol·K), and absolute temperature ($T$ in K) for an ideal gas.
formulas.example: What volume does 2.0 mol $\text{CO}_2$ occupy at 300 K and 1.0 atm? $V = nRT/P = (2.0)(0.0821)(300)/1.0 = 49.3$ L.

formulas.name: Graham's Law of effusion
formulas.description: The ratio of effusion rates of two gases at the same temperature and pressure is inversely proportional to the square root of their molar masses.
formulas.example: $\text{H}_2$ vs. $\text{O}_2$: rate ratio $= \sqrt{32/2} = \sqrt{16} = 4$. $\text{H}_2$ effuses 4× faster than $\text{O}_2$.

formulas.name: Beer-Lambert Law
formulas.description: Absorbance ($A$) is proportional to molar absorptivity ($\varepsilon$ in L/mol·cm), path length ($l$ in cm), and molar concentration ($c$ in mol/L).
formulas.example: If $\varepsilon = 5000$ L/mol·cm, $l = 1.00$ cm, and $c = 2.0 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/L, then $A = 5000 \times 1.00 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-4} = 1.00$.

formulas.name: Molarity
formulas.description: Molarity is moles of solute per liter of solution; the most common unit of concentration in AP Chemistry.
formulas.example: Dissolving 0.500 mol NaCl in 250. mL of solution: $M = 0.500/0.250 = 2.00$ mol/L.

exam_tip: Real gas deviations are tested frequently. At high pressure, volume is underestimated by the ideal gas law (actual volume is larger due to finite molecular size). At low temperature, pressure is overestimated (actual pressure is lower due to attractive IMFs slowing molecules). Know which correction goes in which direction.
